quickStart.title: Get productive in 30 seconds
steps: Install the Continue VS Code extension and open the sidebar panel.
Set up at least one model in config.yaml or through the UI for chat/edit/apply, plus an autocomplete-capable model if you want tab completion.
Use Cmd/Ctrl+L to send selected code to chat, or open Agent mode and describe the task in plain English.
Cycle modes with Cmd/Ctrl+. between Chat, Plan, and Agent depending on whether you want conversation, safe planning, or code changes.
Add context with @ mentions like files or terminal output before asking for a fix or explanation.

dailyWorkflows.title: Ask about selected code fast
dailyWorkflows.description: Send your current selection to chat and iterate conversationally.
dailyWorkflows.command: Select code → Cmd/Ctrl+L → ask for explanation, tests, or refactors.

dailyWorkflows.title: Plan safely before editing
dailyWorkflows.description: Use Plan mode when you want repo exploration and reasoning without write tools.
dailyWorkflows.command: Cmd/Ctrl+. until Plan mode is active, then ask for an implementation plan and impacted files.

dailyWorkflows.title: Delegate implementation in Agent mode
dailyWorkflows.description: Give Agent a concrete natural-language change request and let it choose tools.
dailyWorkflows.command: Switch to Agent mode and ask: Update every ESLint config to disable @typescript-eslint/naming-convention.

dailyWorkflows.title: Stay in flow with autocomplete
dailyWorkflows.description: Use tab autocomplete for boilerplate, then escalate to chat or agent only when you need larger context or edits.
dailyWorkflows.command: Accept with Tab, reject with Esc, partial accept with Cmd/Ctrl+Right, force suggestion in VS Code with Cmd/Ctrl+Alt+Space.

powerMoves.title: Split models by role
powerMoves.description: Configure different models for chat/edit/apply/autocomplete instead of forcing one provider to do everything.
powerMoves.command: In config.yaml, assign roles so a strong reasoning model handles chat/edit while a low-latency model handles autocomplete.
powerMoves.why: This usually improves cost, latency, and suggestion quality all at once.

powerMoves.title: Add external context through providers and MCP
powerMoves.description: Use Continue context providers, including MCP-backed resources, so the model can pull structured context instead of guessing.
powerMoves.command: Configure context providers in Continue and then add them with @ mentions before asking for changes.
powerMoves.why: Better context reduces hallucinations and leads to more actionable edits.

powerMoves.title: Use built-in slash commands for routine repo chores
powerMoves.description: Lean on slash commands for commit messages, review passes, terminal command generation, or issue drafting.
powerMoves.command: /commit, /review, /cmd, or /draftIssue in chat when the task matches.
powerMoves.why: They remove prompt-writing overhead for repetitive tasks.

gotchas.problem: Agent and Plan modes can appear unsupported with some models/providers.
gotchas.solution: If you see Not Supported, switch to a model/provider with tool support or reassign roles in config.yaml.

gotchas.problem: Autocomplete quality suffers if you only configure a chat model and ignore latency-sensitive setup.
gotchas.solution: Use a model/provider that explicitly supports autocomplete and keep it separate from your heavier chat model when possible.

gotchas.problem: Agent mode prompts for tool permission by default, which can feel slow.
gotchas.solution: Use tool policies to auto-allow only the specific tools you trust; do not blanket-approve everything.

gotchas.problem: Context quality is heavily dependent on what you attach.
gotchas.solution: Use selection send-to-chat, @Files, @Terminal, and relevant context providers before asking for edits or debugging help.

gotchas.problem: Continue supports many model providers, so it is easy to misconfigure roles or secrets.
gotchas.solution: Keep config.yaml tidy, use provider-specific API keys/secrets correctly, and verify each model has the right roles assigned.

slashCommands.description: Generate a commit message from the current diff.
slashCommands.tip: Useful right before you stage or commit changes.

slashCommands.description: Review code and give focused feedback on readability, maintainability, smells, and performance.
slashCommands.example: /review Refactor the following function for clarity and performance.
slashCommands.tip: Paste or attach the relevant code context first for better reviews.

slashCommands.description: Generate a terminal command for the task you describe.
slashCommands.example: /cmd find all large files over 50MB in this repo
slashCommands.tip: Handy when you know the outcome you want but not the exact shell incantation.

slashCommands.description: Draft a GitHub issue from the discussed bug or feature request.
slashCommands.example: /draftIssue Summarize the flaky integration test problem as a reproducible bug report.
slashCommands.tip: Good after a debugging session or postmortem.

slashCommands.description: Work with HTTP/API-oriented prompts from chat.
slashCommands.example: /http Explain this endpoint contract and propose a request example.
slashCommands.tip: Best when paired with pasted API details or selected code.

slashCommands.description: Generate onboarding-oriented help for understanding a project.
slashCommands.example: /onboard Help me understand the architecture and main entry points of this repo.
slashCommands.tip: Great first command in an unfamiliar codebase.

slashCommands.description: Share the current conversation or result when supported.
slashCommands.tip: Useful for passing an explanation or review result to teammates.

config.description: List of configured models/providers in config.yaml, each with provider, model name, credentials, and roles.
config.default: No useful behavior until you configure at least one model
config.recommended: Define separate chat/edit/apply and autocomplete models instead of one-size-fits-all.

config.description: Assign a model to roles like chat, edit, apply, embeddings, and autocomplete depending on provider support.
config.recommended: Give autocomplete a fast low-latency model and reserve premium reasoning models for chat/edit.

config.description: Continue supports providers such as Anthropic, OpenAI, Gemini, Ollama, Azure, Bedrock, OpenRouter, LM Studio, and many more.
config.recommended: Pick providers based on hosting, latency, privacy, and capability rather than brand alone.

config.description: Permission behavior for Agent mode tools.
config.recommended: Auto-allow only a narrow trusted subset; leave risky tools approval-gated.

config.description: Sources that can supply structured context to chat or agent, including MCP-backed resources.
config.recommended: Add the providers your team actually uses instead of overloading prompts with pasted context.

config.description: Turns inline code suggestions on or off in the IDE.
config.recommended: Keep on for boilerplate-heavy work; turn off temporarily if suggestions are distracting.

proTips: Chat for exploration, Plan for safe reasoning, Agent for action; switching modes is faster than overprompting in the wrong one.
Use Cmd/Ctrl+L habitually on selected code instead of re-describing code by hand.
When Agent feels weak, the issue is often missing context or a model without tool support, not the prompt itself.
A clean config.yaml with role-specific models is one of the highest leverage Continue upgrades.
